title: Quiz: Quantum Importance Sampling
description: Test your understanding of importance sampling techniques in quantum computing

questions.question: What is the main purpose of importance sampling in Monte Carlo methods?

options.text: To increase the number of samples
To reduce variance by sampling from a better distribution
To eliminate bias in estimates
To speed up random number generation

questions.explanation: Importance sampling reduces variance by sampling from a proposal distribution that places more probability on 'important' regions where |f(x)| is large, then reweighting to correct for the changed distribution.

questions.question: What is the optimal importance sampling proposal distribution q*(x) for estimating E_p[f(X)]?

options.text: q*(x) = p(x)
q*(x) proportional to |f(x)| * p(x)
q*(x) = uniform distribution
q*(x) proportional to 1/|f(x)|

questions.explanation: The optimal proposal is q*(x) proportional to |f(x)| * p(x). This places more probability where the contribution to the integral is largest, achieving zero variance in the ideal case.

questions.question: In quantum importance sampling, what is the importance weight used to correct samples drawn from proposal distribution q?

options.text: w(x) = q(x) / p(x)
w(x) = p(x) / q(x)
w(x) = sqrt(p(x) / q(x))
w(x) = p(x) * q(x)

questions.explanation: The importance weight is w(x) = p(x) / q(x), which corrects for the fact that we sampled from q instead of the target p. This ensures the reweighted estimate is unbiased.

questions.question: What does the effective sample size (ESS) measure in importance sampling?

options.text: The total number of samples drawn
The equivalent number of independent samples from the target
The variance of the function f
The difference between proposal and target

questions.explanation: The effective sample size measures how many independent samples from the target distribution the importance-weighted samples are equivalent to. Low ESS indicates weight degeneracy and poor proposal choice.

questions.question: In quantum computing, amplitude amplification can be used to implement a form of importance sampling by increasing the probability of measuring important states.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Amplitude amplification (related to Grover's algorithm) can selectively boost the amplitudes of 'important' states, effectively implementing a form of importance sampling by changing the measurement distribution.

questions.question: When is self-normalized importance sampling used instead of standard importance sampling?

options.text: When the function f is unbounded
When the target distribution is not normalized (unknown normalizing constant)
When we have too many samples
When the proposal equals the target

questions.explanation: Self-normalized importance sampling is used when the target distribution p(x) is known only up to a normalizing constant. The ratio of weighted sums cancels the unknown constant.

questions.question: In VQE, how can importance sampling reduce measurement overhead?

options.text: By reducing the number of qubits needed
By allocating more shots to Pauli terms with larger coefficients
By eliminating the need for classical optimization
By preparing better initial states

questions.explanation: In VQE, importance sampling allocates measurement shots proportionally to |coefficient| * variance for each Pauli term, focusing resources on terms that contribute most to the total variance.

questions.question: Which of the following are challenges with importance sampling? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Choosing a good proposal distribution requires knowledge of f
Weight degeneracy when proposal is very different from target
It always increases variance compared to direct sampling
High-dimensional problems can have severe weight degeneracy

questions.explanation: Choosing a good proposal needs knowledge of f, weight degeneracy occurs when proposal differs significantly from optimal, and high dimensions worsen this. However, good IS always reduces variance compared to direct sampling.
